coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from postconsumer waste silk cloth and investigation of its electron transfer dynamics with methyl viologen dichloride
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Indian Chemical Society
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Indian Chemical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Indian Chemical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Vadivel, Ramanan
Nirmala, Muthukumaran
Raji, Kaviyarasan
Siddaiah, Bommana
Ramamurthy, Perumal
coredata.dc:description: Synthesis of luminescent carbon dots (CDs) from biological waste materials is gaining more attention in the present-day scenario. We have synthesized highly luminescent (luminescence quantum yield, φ ​= ​19.1%), water-soluble CDs from a postconsumer waste silk cloth via a facile hydrothermal synthetic method. The resulting CDs are characterized and their photophysical properties are studied in detail. The electron transfer dynamics of CDs in presence of methyl viologen dichloride hydrate (MV2+) is systematically investigated in this work. Knowledge of the electron transfer dynamics of CDs is essential in the structural elucidation of CDs, prediction of sensing mechanisms and utilizing the CDs in energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrothermal synthesis
Dynamic quenching
Stern-Volmer analysis

Investigation of electron transfer dynamics between CDs and MV2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) histogram showing the size distribution, (c) Raman spectrum, and (d) FTIR spectrum of the CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, (b) excitation wavelength dependent emission spectrum, (c) Peak normalized excitation wavelength dependent emission spectrum, (d) excitation spectrum (λem ​= ​446 ​nm), (e) 2D fluorescence contour map, and (d) luminescence quantum yield determination of CDs.
Luminescence decay profiles of CDs at various emission monitoring wavelengths. IRF – Instrument Response Function (50 ps), λex ​= ​375 ​nm.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs in presence of various concentrations of MV2+(purple colour spectrum is the absorption spectrum of 40 ​mM ​MV2+ alone without CDs), (b) emission spectra of CDs in presence of various concentrations of MV2+and (c) the corresponding S–V plot. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) Luminescence decay profiles of CDs in presence of various concentrations of MV2+and (b) the corresponding S–V plot. (λex ​= ​375 ​nm, λem ​= ​446 ​nm).
Fitted parameters of luminescence decay profiles of CDs at various emission monitoring wavelengths. λex ​= ​375 ​nm.
Fitted parameters of luminescence decay profiles of CDs at various emission monitoring wavelengths. (λex ​= ​375 ​nm, λem ​= ​446 ​nm).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from postconsumer waste silk cloth and investigation of its electron transfer dynamics with methyl viologen dichloride

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Forensic Sciences Department, 30A Kamarajar Salai, Government of Tamil Nadu, Chennai, 600004, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology - Madras, Chennai, 600036, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. National Centre for Ultrafast Processes, University of Madras, Taramani Campus, Chennai, 600113, Tamil Nadu, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of luminescent carbon dots (CDs) from biological waste materials is gaining more attention in the present-day scenario. We have synthesized highly luminescent (luminescence quantum yield, φ ​= ​19.1%), water-soluble CDs from a postconsumer waste silk cloth via a facile hydrothermal synthetic method. The resulting CDs are characterized and their photophysical properties are studied in detail. The electron transfer dynamics of CDs in presence of methyl viologen dichloride hydrate (MV2+) is systematically investigated in this work. Knowledge of the electron transfer dynamics of CDs is essential in the structural elucidation of CDs, prediction of sensing mechanisms and utilizing the CDs in energy storage devices.

ce:section.ce:para: It is known fact that the fundamental use of textiles is to shield the body from climatic changes and to conserve modesty. Apart from that, they reflect prosperity, wealth, luxury life, personality, or fashion interests of people. But in the present scenario, “fashion waste” due to “fast fashion” is a serious threat to the environment [1]. Fast fashion trend results in the overconsumption of textiles, where consumers buy more than they require, which leads to piling up of postconsumer textile wastes. It has been estimated that these wastes that go to landfills are about 3.5 million tonnes in the United Kingdom and 10.5 million tonnes in the United States [2]. The global postconsumer textile wastes discarded per year has been estimated to be about 92 million tonnes and the expected wastes that would be generated by the year 2030 is more than 134 million tonnes per year! [3] These waste garment materials also include age-old and out-of-fashioned silk cloths [4]. Some amounts of these postconsumer silk wastes are being recycled. At the same time, it is to be noted that only 20% of postconsumer textile waste is collected for reuse or recycling. Of them, less than 1% of materials are recycled into new clothing [5]. In this scenario, establishing alternative strategies to manage these waste materials and to convert them to useful products is the need of the hour.

In this context, as an initiative, we have demonstrated the synthesis of luminescent carbon dots (CDs) from postconsumer silk waste by a facile hydrothermal synthetic method. CDs are identified as new zero-dimensional materials in the nanocarbon family that fluoresce inherently and are usually less than 10 ​nm in size [6–8]. CDs can easily be synthesized from various precursors such as organic molecules, polymers, carbonaceous bulky materials, and even from environmental wastes [6–11]. Among these precursors, the environmental waste materials gaining more attention under the shades of green and sustainable chemistry. We know that carbon is an abundant element and it serves as the basic building block of all organisms. Since the CDs are carbon-based, they exhibit low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility. Hence, they can be used as a potential alternative to the conventional heavy metal-based inorganic quantum dots in drug delivery, bioimaging and nanomedicine applications [7–9,12,13]. Quantum dots are efficient candidates in the fields of luminescent sensors, photocatalysis, optoelectronics, etc. [6–14] owing to their fascinating features such as high luminescence quantum yield (φ), chemical inertness, exhibition of stable luminescence under various solution environments (like different ionic strengths and pH), photostability, excitation wavelength dependent luminescence and smaller size.

Wang et al. [15] and Ramanan et al. [16] have reported that the photoexcited CDs could act as both excellent electron donor and electron acceptor. Studies on the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) properties of CDs are very important to utilize the CDs in solar energy conversion and related applications [14,16]. Apart from that the knowledge on the PET properties could be valuable in the mechanistic and structural elucidation of CDs [15,17]. However, exclusive studies on the PET behavior of CDs are only very limited [15–19]. In the present study, we have comprehensively studied the PET process between the postconsumer silk waste-derived CDs and methyl viologen dication (MV2+). Since, the reduction potential of MV2+ (−0.44 ​V vs NHE) is comparable to the conduction band potential of TiO2, –0.5 ​V vs NHE, (a semiconductor material widely used in dye-sensitized solar cells, DSSCs), understanding the PET process between the CDs and the MV2+ could offer new insights in developing the CDs-based energy conversion devices.

A waste, old silk dhoti was used as the precursor to obtaining the luminescent CDs. Methyl viologen dichloride hydrate was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Orthophosphoric acid and sodium hydroxide pellets were purchased from Qualigens. Spectra/Por Float-A-Lyzer G2 cellulose ester dialysis membrane with MWCO of 1 KD was purchased from Spectrum Labs. Water was purified using Aquelix 5 water purification system, and used throughout the work.

The waste silk cloth was slashed into pieces and 0.5 ​g of it was taken in a 100 ​mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave followed by the addition of 20 ​mL of 20% o-phosphoric acid as oxidizing cum passivating agent. The autoclave was then sealed and kept in a muffle furnace at 250 ​°C for 5 ​h which yielded a yellow colored solution, indicated the formation of CDs. The solution was filtered to remove unreacted silk cloth remnants. The pH of the obtained yellow colored solution was found to be more acidic which indicates the presence of excess phosphoric acid. To remove the excess phosphoric acid, the yellow solution was dried and re-dissolved in ethanol followed by the drop-wise addition of dil. NaOH solution with stirring. The solution pH was monitored during the neutralization process and the addition of dil. NaOH was continued until the attainment of the neutralization point. The white colour precipitate of trisodium phosphate was filtered using a Whatman 40 grade filter paper. The resulting filtrate was then centrifuged at 15,000 ​rpm for 20 ​min to remove any larger particles. Traces of trisodium phosphate, smaller molecules and/or polymer-like byproducts that formed due to partial carbonization were removed by dialysis using a dialysis membrane of 1 KD MWCO for 48 ​h. The resulting clear solution was vacuum dried, re-dissolved in de-ionized water and used for further studies.

The morphology and size of the CDs were examined using FEI Tecnai-G2 TEM instrument. Raman spectrum was recorded using a Laser Raman microscope-RAMAN 11i (Nanophoton Corp., Japan). The excitation wavelength used was 532 ​nm. The nature of surface functional groups on the CDs was probed with a Bruker, Vertex 70 FT-IR spectrometer at attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Instruments and methods employed for photophysical investigation
ce:section.ce:para:
Cary 100 Bio UV–vis double beam spectrophotometer was employed to record the UV–vis absorption spectrum. HORIBA JOBIN YVON Fluoromax 4 ​P spectrofluorimeter was utilized to obtain the emission, excitation and 2D contour topographical map of CDs. The luminescence decay profiles were recorded using time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique with microchannel plate photomultiplier tube (MCP-PMT) as a detector and a laser with excitation wavelength of 375 ​nm (FWHM: 50 ps) as the excitation source. The TCSPC datawere analyzed with the software provided by IBH (DAS-6), which is based on reconvolution technique using iterative non-linear least square methods. The quality of the fit is recognized by the reduced χ2 value. The obtained decay profiles were best fitted with the tri-exponential function and the average lifetimes (τavg) of photon emission were calculated according to the following equation.
τ
a v g
=
[
∑
α i
τ i 2
∑
α i
τ i
]
where the αi denotes the fractional weights of the various decay time components, τi of the tri-exponential fitting.

The luminescence quantum yield (φ) of CDs was measured by the slope method using quinine sulfate (with the known φ of 0.543 ​at the excitation of 355 ​nm) as the reference standard. 0.1 ​N ​H2SO4 was used as the solvent for quinine sulfate whereas Millipore water was employed as the solvent for the CDs. The following formula was used to calculate the φ of CDs.
φ
C D s
=
φ
Q S
[
K
C D s
K
Q S
]
[
n
C D s
n
Q S
]
2
where ‘K’ represents the slope obtained by plotting the integrated area of the corresponding emission against absorbance. ‘n’ denotes the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript ‘QS’ refers to the standard quinine sulfate. For emission quenching experiments, the CDs were excited at 375 ​nm.

TEM image of the waste silk-derived CDs is shown in Fig. 1 a which reveals the spherical morphology of the as-prepared CDs. The statistical diameter of the CDs is 6.1 ​± ​2.2 ​nm as computed from the TEM image by analyzing 25 particles. The corresponding particle size distribution histogram is presented in Fig. 1b. The histogram also reveals a narrow particle size distribution of the CDs.

Raman spectrum is the potential spectroscopic tool to estimate the amorphicity and crystallinity of the CDs. To estimate the same, Raman spectrum was recorded for the CDs and is shown in Fig. 1c. The obtained Raman spectrum was fitted with Gaussian function and de-convoluted into two bands with maxima at 1342 and 1547 ​cm−1. The former band corresponds to ‘D’ band which represents the oscillations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite. Thus, these carbons are sp3 hybridized. The latter band is attributed to the ‘G’ band, whcih corresponds to the E2g mode of the graphite and is associated with the vibration of sp2 carbons in a two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal lattice. The intensity ratio between these two bands (ID/IG) could give information about the solid-state nature of the CDs. The calculated ID/IG value is 1.84 which reveals that the CDs are ‘diamond-like’ in nature i.e. it possesses 1.84 times higher sp3 carbons than the sp2 carbons. Hence, the resulting CDs should be amorphous in nature. The high magnitude of the ID/IG value further indicates that the CDs are highly functionalized which provides a reason for the excellent water solubility of CDs.

The major constituent of silk is fibroin protein which contributes about 70–80% to the structure of silk [20]. The primary structure of fibroin majorly consists of the recurrent amino acid sequence (Gly-Ser-Gly-Ala-Gly-Ala)n [21]. This sequence could be broken into amino acids during hydrothermal pyrolysis. The presence of phosphoric acid could assist the cleavage of di-peptide bonds. It is noteworthy that amino acids are reported as excellent precursors to synthesize luminescent CDs [22,23]. Hence, the resulting CDs may contain amine, carboxylic acid, and amide functional groups on the surface. The nature of surface functional groups present in the waste silk-derived CDs were analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy. The FTIR spectrum of CDs is presented in Fig. 1d. The FTIR spectrum of waste silk-derived CDs somewhat resemble the FTIR spectrum of glycine-derived CDs [16]. This resemblance can be justified by looking at the amino acid sequence of the fibroin protein. The sequence contains about 50% of glycine. In the FTIR spectrum of waste silk-derived CDs, the broad absorption from about 3000 to 3650 ​cm−1 is attributed to the collective contributions of –OH, –NH2, and –CH3 stretching vibrations. The strong absorption at about 1680 ​cm−1 is attributed to the CO stretching vibration of carboxylic acid or amide functional groups. Another absorption band from about 1050 to 1450 ​cm−1 is assigned to the collective absorptions of C–O, C–N, OC–O symmetric stretching vibrations and O–H bending vibrations. Finally, the carbonization peak appears at about 575 ​cm−1.

UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs is shown in Fig. 2 a which exhibits a band at 275 ​nm and a hump at 362 ​nm. The former band is attributed to π→π∗ transition of ‘graphite-like’ core region of CDs and the latter hump is assigned to n→π∗ transition of heteroatomic functional groups such as –OH, –NH2, OC–O, –CO–NH2, etc. located at the surface of the CDs [24]. Fig. 2b represents the excitation wavelength dependent emission spectra of CDs which is the most common property of CDs. The corresponding peak normalized emission spectra are shown in Fig. 2c clearly depicts a bathochromic shift of emission maxima as we increase the excitation wavelength in 20 ​nm intervals. This excitation wavelength dependency may be due either to the diversity in the particle size of CDs or to the diverse surface functional groups (defects) at the surface of the CDs. Maximum emission intensity was observed at 446 ​nm when exciting the CDs at 360 ​nm. The excitation spectrum of the CDs (emission monitored at 446 ​nm) is shown in Fig. 2d which shows two excitation peaks at about 260 ​nm and at about 365 ​nm corresponding to core and surface excitations respectively. The excitation peak maxima more or less match with the absorption bands shown in Fig. 2a. 2D contour topographical map of CDs is shown in Fig. 2e which exhibits two contours with excitation maxima at about 260 ​nm and 365 ​nm. It is noteworthy that the emission maxima in the contours are observed almost at the same region for both core and surface excitations which is about 450 ​nm for core excitation and 446 ​nm for surface excitation. This indicates that even though the excitons are generated at the core of the CDs they do not recombine at the core.

Instead, they are trapped at the surface defects and relax to the ground state along with the excitons generated at the surface. The large Stokes shift of about 53,763 ​cm−1 was observed for the core excitation further endorse this supposition [24]. In addition, the areas of the contours are similar in magnitudes which show that the core and surface excitations of the CDs contribute almost equally to the overall emission intensity observed.

The fluorescence quantum yield (φ) of the CDs is calculated to be about 19.1% (Fig. 2f) which is impressive when compared with the quantum yields of most of the reported biowaste-derived CDs which are usually less than 10% [25]. This high φ value extends the materialistic applications of these CDs.

The luminescence lifetimes of the CDs were measured at various emission monitoring wavelengths (λex ​= ​375 ​nm) using the TCSPC technique and the corresponding luminescence decay profiles are shown in Fig. 3 . The decay profiles best-fitted to a triexponential function and the corresponding fitted parameters are presented in Table 1 . The average lifetime is roughly 6 ns and is almost constant from 420 ​nm to 540 ​nm region of emission monitoring wavelength. After that, the average lifetime decreases and becomes 3.97 ns at the emission monitoring wavelength of 580 ​nm. The reduced chi-square (χR 2) values show the good quality of the fit.

To assess the solution state stability of the CDs, the CDs solution was kept in a storage viol in laboratory ambient lighting conditions. The emission intensity and the emission maxima of the CDs solution were measured once in 15 days for the total monitoring period of 90 days. During the monitoring period, the emission intensity and the emission maxima of the CDs solution have not displayed any significant change which vindicates the absence of any aggregation or agglomeration of the CDs on long-term storage. Hence, the CDs possess good storage stability and photostability (at ambient lighting). The rich surface functionalization of the CDs could be the reason for the colloidal stability of the CDs solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of electron transfer dynamics between CDs and MV2+
ce:section.ce:para:
The electron-donating property of the CDs is investigated in the presence of MV2+, a well-known electron acceptor. Fig. 4 a shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs in presence of various concentrations (0–33 ​mM) of MV2+. The corresponding emission spectra were exhibiting a regular decrease in the emission intensity of the CDs as shown in Fig. 4b. The observed luminescence quenching may be either due to energy transfer or electron transfer. However, the energy transfer mechanism is not feasible due to the lack of overlap between the absorption spectrum of MV2+ and the emission spectrum of CDs. Hence, the quenching may be due to the electron transfer process. There are two types of electron transfer processes namely static and dynamic electron transfers. The quenching is called static quenching when a non-emissive ground state complex is formed between the fluorophore and the quencher. Dynamic quenching or collisional quenching is the quenching that resulted from diffusive encounters between the fluorophore and quencher during the lifetime of the excited state. Therefore, this is a time-dependent process [26]. To get a clear insight on the electron transfer process operating between the CDs and MV2+, we have carried out Stern-Volmer (S–V) analysis. The S–V equation is given below.
I 0
I
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
where I0 and I are the emission maxima in the absence and the presence of various concentrations of MV2+ respectively. [Q] is the concentration of the MV2+ (quencher). KSV is the S–V quenching constant.

The plot of I0/I against the concentration of MV2+ yields a straight line with a slope equal to 61.3 M− ​1 as shown in Fig. 4c. This observation clearly explains that the quenching process is either purely static or purely dynamic but not the combination of both in which a concave upward deviation would be observed in the S–V plot. [26,27] Now, our task is to determine whether the quenching process is static or dynamic. Both the quenching processes follow second-order kinetics since both the fluorophore and the quencher involve in the rate-determining step of the quenching process. Calculation of the corresponding bimolecular quenching rate constant (kq) would deliver further information about the nature of the quenching process. Hence, we have calculated the same using the following formula.
k q
=
K
S V
τ 0
where τ0 is the lifetime of the CDs in the absence of quencher i.e. MV2+. The calculated kq value is 9.7 ​× ​109 ​M-1s-1 which is very close to the diffusion-controlled limit (1 ​× ​1010 ​M-1s-1). For static quenching, the apparent kq value usually exceeds the diffusion-controlled limit [10,26]. Hence, the observed quenching process, in this case, might be dynamic in nature.

Lifetime quenching analysis is the appropriate method to determine the nature of the quenching process. Hence, we have applied S–V analysis for the lifetime quenching process of CDs in presence of MV2+. Luminescence decay profiles (λem ​= ​446 ​nm) of the CDs in presence of various concentrations of MV2+ are shown in Fig. 5 a. Fig. 5a shows a regular decrease in the luminescence lifetime of the CDs on increasing the concentration of MV2+ (0–33 ​mM). The average lifetime of the CDs in the absence of MV2+ is 6.33 ns. The lifetime keeps on decreasing from 6.33 ns to 2.43 ns on increasing the concentration of MV2+ from 0 ​mM to 33 ​mM (Table 2 ). S–V equation for a lifetime quenching process is given below.
τ 0
τ
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
where τ0 and τ are the average lifetimes of the CDs in the absence and in the presence of various concentrations of MV2+ respectively. The plot of τ0/τ against the concentration of MV2+ yields a straight line with a slope equal to 45.9 M− 1 as shown in Fig. 5b. The corresponding bimolecular quenching rate constant, kq was calculated to be 7.3 ​× ​109 ​M-1s-1 which is again very close to the diffusion-controlled limit. Hence, it is established without any further doubt that the photoinduced electron transfer reaction between the waste silk cloth-derived CDs and MV2+ is purely dynamic in nature.

Highly luminescent CDs were synthesized from postconsumer silk cloth waste by hydrothermal synthetic approach. On characterization analysis, the CDs were found to be in the size of about 6 ​nm and possess carboxylic, amine, and amide functional groups on their surface. The photophysical properties of the CDs were examined thoroughly. The luminescence quantum yield of the CDs is calculated to be 19.1% which is higher when compared with other biowaste-derived CDs. With the intention of utilizing these CDs in energy storage devices, its electron transfer dynamics with methyl viologen dichloride hydrate was investigated in detail. During the investigation, it is found that the photoinduced electron transfer process between the CDs and MV2+ is purely dynamic in nature and the bimolecular quenching rate constant is calculated as 7.3 ​× ​109 ​M-1s-1using Stern-Volmer analysis.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors Dr. M.N. and Prof. P.R. thanks Dr. D. S. Kothari postdoctoral fellowship (DSKPDF- Fellow No. CH/18–19/0177) scheme for financial support in the form of fellowship. Dr. V.R. thanks the Director, Mr. M. Srinivasan, the Deputy Director, Mrs. A. Visalakshi, and the Assistant Director, Mr. K. Manivannan, Forensic Sciences Department, Government of Tamil Nadu, Chennai for their support. The authors thank Dr. A. Karthikieyani, Assistant Professor, Department of Chemistry, Guru Nanak College, Chennai and Dr. T. Senthil Kumar, Chemist, Tamil Nadu Industrial Subordinate services for their suggestions to revise the manuscript.
